column4: Sergio Antonio Alcalá-Corona, Santiago Sandoval-Motta, Jesús Espinal-Enríquez, Enrique Hernández-Lemus
column5: Modularity in Biological Networks
column9: Graph-based / community detection / network analysis (multiple methods reviewed)
column10: Modules are groups of biological entities (genes, proteins, metabolites) more densely interconnected with each other than with the rest of the network. The review surveys structural definitions (community structure), functional definitions (shared biological function), and their relationship. Different algorithms operationalize different aspects of this general definition. Modules may be hierarchical and overlapping depending on the method used.
column11: Gene regulatory networks, protein interaction networks, metabolic networks, and co-expression networks. The review is comprehensive and applicable to any type of biological network.
column12: To provide a comprehensive survey of modularity concepts, detection algorithms, and biological significance across multiple network types.
column13: No; review paper comparing existing algorithms and their applications.
column14: No new experimental data; reviews published network datasets and algorithmic approaches.
column17: Moderate to high impact. Comprehensive contemporary review useful as a one-stop reference for the current state of modularity analysis across biological network types.
column19: This comprehensive review surveys the concept of modularity across diverse biological network types — gene regulatory, protein interaction, metabolic, co-expression — comparing algorithmic approaches and their biological interpretations.

column4: Uri Alon
column5: Network motifs: theory and experimental approaches
column9: Graph-based regulatory networks; randomized-network motif detection; dynamical models of transcription circuits
column10: The paper treats modules as network motifs: recurring regulation patterns or circuits that occur much more often than expected in randomized networks and can be viewed as basic building blocks of transcription networks. These motifs are directed subgraphs identified structurally but interpreted functionally, and they can overlap and combine into larger organizations such as multi-output feedforward loops, SIMs, DORs, feedback loops, and cascades.
column11: Primarily evolved transcription regulation networks in sensory and developmental contexts, with extensions to signaling and neuronal networks. Motifs are used to relate network topology to information-processing function using interaction maps and dynamic experiments.
column12: To identify basic circuit building blocks in biological regulation and explain how recurrent motif structure supports specific information-processing tasks such as delay, pulse generation, response acceleration, memory, and temporal ordering.
column13: Yes; network motifs are detected by comparing subgraph frequencies in observed networks against randomized networks, and their functions are analyzed with mathematical models and tested experimentally.
column14: Uses regulatory-network topology, comparative network data across organisms, fluorescent reporter time-series, and microarray-based transcription dynamics to identify and validate motif functions.
column16: multiple (E. coli, S. cerevisiae, B. subtilis, plants, animals)
column17: Highly influential review that consolidated network motif theory with experimental validation and helped shape systems biology, transcription-network analysis, and synthetic gene circuit design.
column19: This review synthesizes theory and experiments on network motifs as recurring circuit patterns in transcription networks. It argues that motifs are basic building blocks with characteristic information-processing functions and surveys how they are identified, modeled, and validated across diverse organisms.
column20: A.2 Structural Definitions: Network Motifs as Elementary Modules

column5: A module of negative feedback regulators defines growth factor signaling
column9: Signaling network / kinetic systems analysis / network motifs
column10: A module is a kinetically defined, coexpressed cluster of delayed early genes that functions as a negative-feedback unit attenuating growth factor signaling. In this paper, the module is defined primarily by shared temporal behavior and shared regulatory function rather than by a purely graph-theoretic partition. The authors also interpret the signaling network through smaller architectural motifs such as negative autoregulation and coherent/incoherent feed-forward loops.
column11: Mammalian growth-factor signaling, especially EGFR signaling in epithelial cells (HeLa and MCF10A). The module is embedded in a signaling-transcription network and is treated as a conserved systems-level architecture for pathway attenuation and temporal control.
column12: To explain how growth-factor responses are made transient and robust by transcription-dependent feedback, and to identify the regulators that delimit the temporal window of signaling and transcription.
column13: yes - time-course clustering/order by peak expression time, reverse-phase protein lysate array and microarray profiling, interaction-map construction, tumor-compendium/module analysis, and motif-based network interpretation
column14: Time-series phosphoprotein data (RPPA), gene-expression microarrays, qPCR, luciferase assays, immunoprecipitation, siRNA perturbations, and human tumor expression compendia with outcome data.

column19: The paper shows that EGFR signaling induces waves of transcription that include a delayed early cluster of negative regulators. The authors argue that this cluster forms a structured module that attenuates MAPK signaling and immediate-early transcription, and they interpret the response using network-motif logic.

column5: Design Patterns of Biological Cells
column9: Graph-based / network analysis / software engineering analogy
column10: Modules are implicitly defined through 'design patterns' — cataloged recurring solution templates for biological organization categorized as creational (processes building the cell), structural (topological layouts of reaction networks), and behavioral (reaction network functions including switching, adaptation, and oscillation). This constitutes an implicit functional-and-structural definition of biological modules organized by their recurring design solution roles. 21 patterns are identified across three categories.
column11: General cell biology, with applications to E. coli central metabolism and S. cerevisiae pheromone signaling. The paper is motivated by evolved biological systems and draws explicit analogies to software engineering design patterns.
column12: To catalog recurring solution templates (design patterns) in cell biology at a higher level of abstraction than individual network motifs, connecting network topology to biological function across diverse systems.
column13: No; the paper is a taxonomy and catalog without algorithmic implementation for pattern detection.

column19: This paper catalogs 21 design patterns in cell biology — creational, structural, and behavioral — as recurring solution templates for biological organization, providing a higher-level abstraction of modular function that connects network topology to biological purpose.

column4: Oguzhan Atay, Andreas Doncic, Jan M. Skotheim
column5: Switch-like Transitions Insulate Network Motifs to Modularize Biological Networks
column9: ODE / dynamical systems / experimental cell biology
column10: A module is a network motif or subnetwork that can be analyzed in functional isolation from the larger network. Insulation requires: (1) switch-like (ultrasensitive) behavior at the module boundary providing timescale separation, and (2) the amplitude of boundary signal change far exceeding prior gradual changes. Modules are dynamically and functionally defined — they are units whose behavior is independent of context because boundaries are insulated by switch-like transitions. Definition is both structural (the boundary interface) and functional (the switch character).
column11: Yeast pheromone signaling and cell cycle (G1/S transition) in S. cerevisiae. An evolved system in which a feedforward loop motif is embedded in the larger cell cycle network. The question is when a small motif can be validly analyzed in isolation.
column12: To show experimentally that switch-like (digital) transitions at network boundaries insulate embedded motifs from context, enabling valid modular analysis — and that disrupting switch character destroys modular behavior.
column13: Yes; experimental perturbations of the G1/S switch using synthetic constructs; quantitative fluorescence microscopy; mathematical modeling of feedforward loop behavior with and without switch insulation.
column14: Empirical data from yeast fluorescence microscopy with engineered perturbations of G1/S switch sharpness; quantitative measurement of motif behavior.
column17: High impact. Provides the biological mechanism for insulation that Del Vecchio formalized mathematically as retroactivity attenuation through timescale separation, bridging theory and experiment on when modular analysis is valid.
column19: This study provides experimental proof that switch-like transitions at network boundaries insulate embedded motifs from their context, enabling valid modular analysis, and that reducing switch character destroys modularity.

column4: Jeff Clune, Jean-Baptiste Mouret, Hod Lipson
column5: The Evolutionary Origins of Modularity
column9: Evolutionary simulation / neural networks / graph analysis
column10: Modules are defined implicitly as subnetworks shaped by selection to minimize inter-module connections while maintaining functional performance. Standard modularity metrics (Q-score) quantify modular organization. Modularity is a structural property of evolved networks reflecting the balance between intra-module cohesion and inter-module sparsity. The definition is structural rather than functional.
column11: Computational neural network evolution experiments, motivated by general biological network architecture. Selection is multi-objective: networks must solve a computational task and minimize connection costs (analogous to the cost of axons, regulatory interactions, or metabolic links).
column12: To demonstrate that minimizing connection costs — a universal selective pressure — is sufficient to drive spontaneous evolution of modular, evolvable networks, providing an alternative to Kashtan and Alon's modularly varying goals hypothesis.
column13: Yes; multi-objective evolutionary algorithm (NSGA-II) selecting for task performance and minimal connection cost; network modularity measured using Q-score.
column14: Computational simulation data from neural network evolution experiments.
column16: generic (computational model, applicable to gene networks, neural networks, metabolic networks)
column17: High impact (>600 citations). Shows that connection cost minimization — a more universal selective pressure — independently drives modularity, complementing Kashtan and Alon's hypothesis. Influential in computational evolution and systems biology.
column19: This study shows that networks evolved under multi-objective selection for performance and minimal connection costs spontaneously become modular and evolvable, offering a direct selective pressure explanation for the evolutionary origin of modularity.

column4: Domitilla Del Vecchio, Alexander J. Ninfa, Eduardo D. Sontag
column5: Modular Cell Biology: Retroactivity and Insulation
column9: ODE / control theory / biochemical network analysis
column10: A module is a gene circuit component with defined input-output signal-response behavior. Retroactivity quantifies modularity failure: when a downstream module connects to an upstream one, it imposes a 'retroactivity to the input' that alters upstream behavior. Modules have defined boundaries (input/output signals) but these fail when inter-module molecular interactions are non-negligible. Insulators are proposed as mechanisms to restore modular behavior. Definition is functional and engineering-inspired.
column11: Natural and synthetic gene regulatory circuits. The core question is when small circuits can be analyzed in isolation and when interconnection invalidates such analysis.
column12: To quantify how connecting gene circuit modules causes retroactivity — a loading effect altering upstream dynamics — and to propose phosphorylation-cycle-based insulation devices restoring modular behavior.
column13: Yes; mathematical analysis of retroactivity using ODE models; derivation of conditions for large and small retroactivity; design of phosphorylation-cycle insulator with feedback amplification.
column14: No new experimental data; theoretical framework validated with analysis of published gene circuit models.
column16: generic (applicable to bacterial and eukaryotic gene circuits)
column17: Very high impact (>300 citations). Foundational paper introducing retroactivity as a formal, quantitative concept in biological modularity. Spawned a major research program on retroactivity measurement, insulation, and modular design in synthetic biology.
column19: This paper introduces retroactivity as a quantitative measure of modularity failure in gene circuits — the loading effect of downstream connections on upstream module dynamics — and proposes phosphorylation-cycle insulation devices to restore modular behavior.

column5: Engineering Principles in Bio-Molecular Systems: From Retroactivity to Modularity
column9: ODE / control theory / input-output systems analysis
column10: Modules are components in a biological input-output system with defined signal interfaces. The paper extends the retroactivity framework into a control-theoretic formalism: retroactivity is cast as a disturbance that must be rejected by an insulating device. Modules have well-defined input and output ports; modularity holds when input-output behavior of each component is preserved upon interconnection. Definition is functional and engineering-inspired.
column11: Engineered and natural biomolecular systems framed through control engineering analogies. Focus is on gene regulatory networks and signaling cascades as input-output systems.
column12: To expand retroactivity theory into a full control-theoretic input-output framework and cast retroactivity attenuation as a disturbance rejection problem, providing design principles for insulated modular biomolecular systems.
column13: No; theoretical framework paper extending Del Vecchio et al. (2008).
column14: No new experimental data; extends the theoretical analysis of Del Vecchio et al. (2008).
column17: Moderate impact. Extends retroactivity theory into a systems-and-control framework, important for synthetic biology and control engineering communities interested in modular biological design.
column19: This paper extends retroactivity theory into a control-theoretic input-output framework, casting retroactivity attenuation as a disturbance rejection problem and providing principles for designing modular biomolecular systems.

column4: Domitilla Del Vecchio
column5: Modularity, Context-Dependence, and Insulation in Engineered Biological Circuits
column10: A module is an engineered biological circuit component with context-independent input-output behavior. The review identifies three sources of context-dependence undermining modularity: retroactivity (loading at interconnection points), resource competition (shared transcriptional/translational machinery), and growth-rate coupling. Insulation strategies are reviewed for each. Definition is functional and engineering-inspired; modularity is a property that must be actively engineered, not assumed.
column11: Engineered synthetic gene circuits primarily in E. coli and S. cerevisiae. Synthesizes a decade of work on achieving reliable modular design in synthetic biology.
column12: To synthesize understanding of how retroactivity, resource competition, and growth-rate coupling cause context-dependence in engineered biological circuits, and to review design strategies restoring modular behavior.
column13: No; review paper synthesizing existing theoretical and experimental work.
column14: No new experimental data; reviews published experimental and theoretical studies on retroactivity, resource competition, and growth coupling.
column16: multiple (E. coli, S. cerevisiae, general synthetic biology)
column17: High impact. Capstone review of a decade of retroactivity and insulation research. Important reference for synthetic biology community on achieving reliable modular design.
column19: This capstone review synthesizes three sources of context-dependence in engineered biological circuits — retroactivity, resource competition, and growth coupling — and reviews design strategies to achieve reliable modularity.

column10: A 'versatile system core' is a conserved molecular module reused across diverse cellular and developmental contexts by being embedded in different regulatory environments. This reframes the tension between modularity and context-dependence: cores are modular (structurally conserved, reusable) but functionally versatile. Definition is structural (conserved molecular assembly) and functional (context-dependent behavior).

column12: To explain why complex systems evolve and persist as hierarchical, nearly decomposable structures. The watchmaker parable argues modular architecture accelerates evolution by enabling stable intermediate forms. Modularity enables independent development and adaptation of subsystems and reduces the combinatorial complexity of system assembly.

column15: Not specific; spans molecular, cellular, organismal, social, and physical systems as illustrative examples.
column17: Foundational and extraordinarily influential across all disciplines studying complexity. Introduced "near-decomposability" and hierarchical modularity as core organizing principles. column19: This foundational essay argues that complex systems in nature and society share a common hierarchical, nearly decomposable architecture in which strong within-module interactions and weak between-module interactions enable rapid evolution and stable adaptive behavior.

column4: Mark E. J. Newman
column5: Modularity and community structure in networks
column9: Graph-based; spectral graph theory; modularity matrix eigenvalue decomposition
column10: A module (community) is a densely connected group of vertices in a network with only sparser connections between groups. Undirected; non-hierarchical (flat partition of the network); non-overlapping (each node belongs to exactly one community). Purely structural/graph-based definition formalized through the modularity quality function Q, which compares the fraction of intra-community edges to that expected in a random graph with the same degree sequence. Modules cannot share elements or be nested in the basic formulation.
column11: General network analysis applicable to social networks, biological networks (metabolic, regulatory), computer networks, and ecological networks. Not specific to one biological setting. Demonstrated on published empirical datasets including social networks and ecological networks. Purpose is community detection methodology.
column12: To detect and characterize community structure (dense subgroups) in complex networks. In biological applications, identified communities correspond to putative functional units in metabolic or regulatory networks. The modularity Q-score quantifies the quality of any proposed partition.
column13: Yes; introduces a spectral algorithm based on the leading eigenvector of the modularity matrix B, where B_ij = A_ij - (k_i * k_j) / 2m. The algorithm partitions networks by iterative bisection: at each step, the leading eigenvector of B is computed and nodes are assigned to two groups based on the sign of their eigenvector components, maximizing the modularity Q.
column14: Uses several published benchmark network datasets for validation: Zachary's karate club, a dolphin social network, NCAA college football scheduling, and a political books co-purchasing network. All are literature-curated empirical datasets.
column15: Not specific to a biological level; the framework is general and domain-agnostic.
column16: None specific (general method demonstrated on social and ecological datasets)
column17: Extremely high impact. One of the most influential papers in network science. The modularity matrix and spectral partitioning approach became standard tools across physics, biology, social science, and computer science. Widely adopted in systems biology for identifying functional modules in gene regulatory, metabolic, and protein-protein interaction networks.
column19: This paper reformulates the network modularity quality function Q in terms of eigenvectors of a modularity matrix, yielding a spectral algorithm for community detection that outperforms competing methods in quality and speed across several published network datasets.

column5: Modularity of biological systems: a link between structure and function
column9: Boolean network; algebraic decomposition theory (direct product and semidirect product of Boolean networks); graph-based (wiring diagram / dependency graph)
column10: A module is a strongly connected component (SCC) of the wiring diagram (dependency graph) of a Boolean network such that the full network decomposes as an iterated semidirect product of sub-networks over these components. Directed; hierarchical (the condensation DAG induces a partial order on modules); non-overlapping (each node belongs to exactly one SCC). The definition explicitly bridges structural and functional perspectives: the structural SCC decomposition induces an analogous decomposition of the state transition graph (dynamics). Not based on density/community detection; based on algebraic decomposition theory.
column11: Gene regulatory networks modeled as Boolean networks. Applies to both random Boolean networks with controlled modularity parameters and published biological Boolean network models. column12: To establish a rigorous mathematical link between structural modularity (SCC decomposition of the wiring diagram) and functional modularity (decomposition of the state transition graph). column13: Yes; modules are identified via strongly connected component decomposition of the wiring diagram, followed by algebraic decomposition of the Boolean network into an iterated semidirect product of component sub-networks. column17: Moderate and growing. Provides the first rigorous mathematical framework formally connecting structural and functional modularity in Boolean networks. The simulation-based evolutionary hypothesis (modularity increases phenotypic complexity while maintaining robustness) contributes to the broader debate on the evolution of modularity.
column19: This paper establishes a mathematical correspondence between structural modularity (SCC decomposition of the wiring diagram) and functional modularity (state-space decomposition) in Boolean networks, and provides simulation evidence that modularity may have evolved to increase phenotypic complexity while preserving dynamic robustness.
